steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The word "however" signals a contrast between two independent clauses. The SAT rule: to join two independent clauses with a conjunctive adverb like "however," use a semicolon before it and a comma after: IC; however, IC.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — comma before "however": Creates a comma splice — two independent clauses cannot be joined by a comma alone. ✗
  • Choice B — no punctuation before "however": Also a comma splice — the clauses run together without a proper separator. ✗
  • Choice C — semicolon before "however": Produces the correct pattern: these consonants are rare; in some dialects of Mandarin, however, four such consonants … are relatively common. ✓
  • Choice D — "and" before "however": Ungrammatical — "and however" is not a valid conjunction. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
